title: Hasbro, Inc. (HAS) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Hasbro, Inc. (HAS) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1. Economic Prospect Score: 60/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 4.0% free cash flow growth assumption is modeled. While Hasbro experienced a massive recent spike in revenue growth (31.3%), a 4% long-term FCF growth rate is highly conservative. It reflects the ongoing challenge of transitioning its massive physical toy business into higher-margin digital segments.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: An 8.0% discount rate is utilized. This cost of capital balances the phenomenal underlying strength of its IP library (Transformers, Monopoly) against the high volatility of the hit-driven entertainment sector it heavily relies upon.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.0% terminal growth rate is applied. This roughly tracks long-term inflation. The physical toy market is mature and arguably structurally declining; assigning a higher perpetual growth rate to the overall enterprise is difficult to mathematically justify.

thesisTitle: The Value Thesis: A Heavy Price for Digital Dreams
thesisParagraphs: At a current price near $91.36, Hasbro's valuation appears stretched relative to its current cash generation. The market is pricing the stock as a successful, high-margin digital entertainment company, yet its current free cash flow of $434M against a $12.8B market cap suggests a business still heavily tethered to its legacy roots.

The core investment debate revolves around the company's IP. The intellectual property is undeniably incredibly valuable. However, a DCF model relies on cash. Until Hasbro can demonstrably and consistently convert its massive brands into structurally higher free cash flow—rather than lumpy, hit-driven spikes—the intrinsic value calculation indicates the stock is overvalued, offering zero margin of safety.
verdictFooter: Based on latest available financial data (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why does the DCF show HAS as OVERVALUED despite a 31% revenue spike?
faq.answer: Revenue spikes in entertainment and licensing are notoriously lumpy. The DCF model anchors its valuation on the actual $434M of free cash flow generated. Projecting a normalized 4% growth rate on that base simply cannot mathematically justify a $12.8B market cap or a $91 share price.

faq.question: Is the massive value of brands like Transformers captured in this DCF?
faq.answer: Indirectly. The DCF captures the value of IP only through the cash it produces. If the IP is incredibly valuable but the company consistently fails to efficiently monetize it into cash flow, the intrinsic value will remain lower than what a pure 'sum-of-the-parts' brand valuation might suggest.

faq.question: What would change the verdict to UNDERVALUED?
faq.answer: If Hasbro fundamentally shifts its business model, permanently accelerating its FCF margin (e.g., generating $800M+ annually) through highly successful digital gaming transitions, the intrinsic value would shift dramatically higher. Alternatively, a significant price correction toward the low $60s would provide a margin of safety.

prospectVerdictDetail: Hasbro's economic prospects are mixed. Its core strength lies in its portfolio of iconic intellectual property (IP), including Monopoly and Transformers. Revenue growth has shown a sharp, volatile rebound (31.3%), heavily dependent on blockbuster entertainment releases and digital gaming success. However, its free cash flow of roughly $434 million on a nearly $13 billion market cap highlights a structurally lower-margin, high-investment business model compared to pure software peers. The ongoing shift from physical toys to digital entertainment remains its primary battleground.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: Competitive momentum is highly volatile, driven by the unpredictable nature of hit-driven entertainment and licensing, offset by the stable underlying performance of classic board games.

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs Peers
factors.rationale: A recent 31.3% spike in revenue growth indicates a successful capitalization on key licensing agreements or a strong theatrical/digital release slate, significantly outpacing traditional toy manufacturing peers.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: The broader physical toy market is stagnant to declining, losing share to digital entertainment. Hasbro's trajectory depends entirely on its ability to pivot its legacy brands into successful digital and multimedia franchises.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: Pricing power on physical toys is constrained by retail consolidation (e.g., Walmart, Target) and consumer sensitivity. However, it commands higher margins and pricing authority within its digital and collectible segments.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: Product velocity is decent, but the cycle times for major theatrical releases or AAA video game integrations involving its IP are long, creating lumpy revenue recognition.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: The moat is built entirely on the intangible value of its legacy brands and its decades of IP development, though physical manufacturing provides little structural advantage.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: There are virtually zero switching costs for consumers buying toys or playing video games. They seamlessly migrate to whatever the current cultural zeitgeist dictates.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: Some of its properties, particularly trading card games or multiplayer digital titles, exhibit strong local network effects, creating highly engaged, sticky communities of players.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: The company's intellectual property portfolio is its crown jewel. Brands like Transformers, Monopoly, and Nerf are deeply ingrained in global pop culture, providing an insurmountable barrier against direct replication.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: Physical toy manufacturing and massive marketing budgets for entertainment releases are highly capital intensive. Generating $434M in FCF relative to its scale indicates a less efficient economic engine than pure digital platforms.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: Sentiment is currently mixed, heavily reliant on the success of near-term product launches and the market's assessment of its digital transformation strategy.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: The lack of consistent earnings growth and a high forward PE ratio (14.6x) suggest analysts remain cautious, viewing recent revenue spikes as potentially one-off rather than structural, leading to mixed estimate revisions.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative oscillates between optimism regarding its digital gaming and licensing divisions and pessimism surrounding the secular decline of its traditional physical toy business.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Management faces the complex task of navigating a massive structural transition. Capital allocation is heavily scrutinized, particularly regarding past acquisitions and the return on investment for major entertainment ventures.

prospectKeyRisks: A secular decline in physical toy sales as children increasingly migrate to digital entertainment and video games at younger ages.
Significant reliance on hit-driven entertainment; a string of poorly received movies or video games utilizing its IP can severely impact near-term financial performance.
Supply chain vulnerabilities and raw material cost inflation, particularly regarding its reliance on overseas manufacturing for physical products.
prospectKeyCatalysts: A highly successful, massive multimedia franchise launch (e.g., a blockbuster movie or AAA video game) that revitalizes a dormant legacy brand.
Accelerated, high-margin growth in its digital gaming and licensing segments, fundamentally shifting the company's revenue mix and valuation multiple.
Strategic monetization or spin-offs of specific, highly valuable IP assets to unlock shareholder value.
prospectMethodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored HAS at 58/100 and Opus at 63/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
